section.title: Continue in the Scriptures
section.sectionRationale: Against the godlessness of the last days, Paul sets a different way: his own tested example of teaching, suffering, and rescue, and above all the Holy Scriptures. He calls Timothy to continue in what he has learned since infancy, then gives the New Testament's clearest statement on the nature and use of Scripture: all of it is God-breathed and profitable, so that the man of God is complete and equipped for every good work.
section.thesis: In contrast to deceivers who go from bad to worse, Timothy must continue in the Scriptures he has known from infancy, which make him wise for salvation through faith in Christ, for all Scripture is God-breathed and profitable to equip the man of God completely for every good work.
section.frameNote: Three movements: Paul's own example of teaching, endurance, and rescue amid persecution; the call to continue in the Scriptures known from infancy, which give saving wisdom; and the great statement that all Scripture is God-breathed and profitable to equip the man of God.

verses.text: You, however, have observed my teaching, my conduct, my purpose, my faith, my patience, my love, my perseverance,
my persecutions, and the sufferings that came upon me in Antioch, Iconium, and Lystra. What persecutions I endured! Yet the Lord rescued me from all of them.
Indeed, all who desire to live godly lives in Christ Jesus will be persecuted,
while evil men and imposters go from bad to worse, deceiving and being deceived.
But as for you, {{meno|continue}} in the things you have learned and firmly believed, since you know from whom you have learned them.
From infancy you have known the {{hieragrammata|Holy Scriptures}}, which are able to make you wise for salvation through faith in Christ Jesus.

units.label: God-breathed and profitable
units.note: the man of God fully equipped

verses.text: All Scripture is {{theopneustos|God-breathed}} and is {{ophelimos|useful}} for instruction, for conviction, for correction, and for training in righteousness,
so that the man of God may be {{artios|complete}}, fully equipped for every good work.

meno.gloss: To remain, abide, continue, stay put. Against imposters who go from bad to worse, Timothy is to do the opposite: not move on to novelty but stay rooted in what he has learned and firmly believed. Faithfulness here is a settled, ongoing abiding in the truth, holding the ground already given rather than drifting with the times.

hieragrammata.gloss: Sacred writings, holy letters, a common term for the Old Testament Scriptures Timothy learned as a child from Lois and Eunice. Paul says these writings are able to make one wise for salvation through faith in Christ Jesus, so the whole of Scripture points to and finds its saving goal in Him. The Bible's purpose is not mere information but saving wisdom.

theopneustos.gloss: God-breathed, breathed out by God. This single compound word, found only here in the New Testament, teaches that Scripture originates in God: it is the product of His own breath, His speech. It grounds the Bible's authority and trustworthiness not in human insight but in divine origin. Because God breathed it out, every part is His word and therefore true and reliable.

ophelimos.gloss: Useful, profitable, beneficial. Because Scripture is God-breathed, it is also supremely practical: profitable for teaching what is true, convicting of what is false, correcting what is wrong, and training in right living. Its four uses cover doctrine and life together, so the Bible is not only authoritative in origin but sufficient in use for shaping godly people.

artios.gloss: Complete, fitted, fully suited for a task. Paired with the phrase fully equipped, it describes the man of God as capable and ready, lacking nothing needed for the work God gives. Scripture is what makes him so: through the God-breathed word he is furnished for every good work, which underlines the Bible's sufficiency for the whole of the Christian life and ministry.

basic.context: Paul contrasts Timothy with the deceivers just described. Timothy has watched Paul's whole life, his teaching, his sufferings, and how the Lord rescued him, and he should expect that godly living brings persecution. Then Paul urges him to keep going in the Scriptures he has known since childhood, which lead to salvation in Christ, and he sums up why: all of the Bible comes from God's own breath and is profitable to make God's servant complete and ready for every good work.
basic.hermeneutics: This passage is itself about how to read and use the Bible. Paul says Scripture is God-breathed, which secures its authority and truth, and profitable for teaching, rebuking, correcting, and training, which shows its practical sufficiency. The goal is not just knowledge but a life fully equipped for good works, so Scripture is meant to be obeyed, not merely studied.
basic.originalLanguages: Timothy is to meno, to remain or continue, in what he learned. He has known the hiera grammata, the sacred writings, from infancy. All Scripture is theopneustos, God-breathed, and ophelimos, useful or profitable, so that the man of God may be artios, complete, fully equipped for every good work.
basic.typology: The Old Testament Scriptures Timothy learned as a child point forward to Christ, making the reader wise for salvation through faith in Him. Paul's rescue from persecution foreshadows the final rescue of all who suffer for godliness.
basic.christ: The Scriptures find their saving purpose in Christ: they make one wise for salvation through faith in Christ Jesus. He is the goal to which all the sacred writings lead, and the faith by which their wisdom is received.
basic.god: God is the author of Scripture, which He breathes out as His own word. This is why the Bible is true and profitable. The same God who inspired the word rescued Paul from every persecution and equips His servants through that word for every good work.
basic.crossReferences: Second Peter 1 says the Scriptures came as men were carried along by the Holy Spirit. Psalm 19 and Psalm 119 celebrate the perfection and profit of God's word. Luke 24 shows the whole of Scripture pointing to Christ.
basic.oneStory: God has spoken, breathing out Scripture to lead His people to salvation in Christ and to equip them for His work. From the writings Timothy learned as a child to the gospel Paul preached, one God-breathed word runs through the whole story, making people wise for salvation and ready for every good work until Christ returns.

context.literary: The movement opens with an emphatic contrast, you, however, setting Timothy against the imposters of verses 1 to 9. Paul appeals first to his own observed example, then to Timothy's lifelong instruction, and finally rises to a doctrinal summit in verses 16 and 17 about the nature and use of Scripture. The flow moves from personal example, to continue in what you know, to the ground of it all, the God-breathed word.
context.historical: Paul recalls specific sufferings at Antioch, Iconium, and Lystra, places Timothy knew well, since Timothy was from that region and joined Paul there. Timothy had been taught the Old Testament from infancy by his mother and grandmother. The false teachers of the day prized novelty and secret knowledge, so Paul's insistence on the settled, sufficient, God-breathed Scriptures cut directly against them.
context.canonical: Verses 16 and 17 are the New Testament's foundational text on the inspiration and sufficiency of Scripture, standing with Second Peter 1:20 to 21. The single word God-breathed has shaped the church's confession that the Bible is the word of God, authoritative and trustworthy in all it teaches.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To anchor Timothy against deception by rooting him in Paul's tested example and, decisively, in the God-breathed Scriptures, showing that the Bible is both authoritative in origin and sufficient in use to make God's servant complete for every good work.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Paul's autobiography and the reminder of Timothy's upbringing are descriptive examples. The commands to continue in the Scriptures and the teaching that all Scripture is God-breathed and profitable are prescriptive and doctrinal, binding the church to receive and use the whole Bible as God's word.

debates.question: Does all Scripture is God-breathed teach the inspiration of every part of the Bible?

views.label: Yes, all Scripture without exception
views.case: The word all with Scripture, along with God-breathed, affirms that every part of the sacred writings originates from God and shares His authority and truthfulness.

views.label: The stress falls on its profitable use
views.case: Some render it every God-breathed Scripture is also profitable, but even this assumes divine inspiration and simply highlights the practical sufficiency that follows from it.

debates.bearing: Either rendering affirms that Scripture comes from God and is profitable; the church confesses both its inspiration and its sufficiency for doctrine and life.

debates.question: What is the scope of the Scripture Paul has in view?

views.label: Primarily the Old Testament
views.case: The sacred writings Timothy knew from infancy were the Hebrew Scriptures, so Paul's immediate reference is the Old Testament, which points to Christ.

views.label: Extending in principle to the apostolic writings
views.case: The New Testament elsewhere places apostolic writings alongside Scripture, so the principle that God-breathed writings are authoritative applies to the whole canon.

debates.bearing: The doctrine of inspiration articulated here undergirds the authority of the entire Bible the church now confesses as God's word.

study.originalLanguages: The movement climbs to its height on four key words. Timothy is to meno, to remain and continue, in what he has learned, the settled abiding that is the opposite of the imposters who drift from bad to worse. He has known from infancy the hiera grammata, the sacred writings, able to make him wise for salvation through faith in Christ. Then comes the summit: all Scripture is theopneustos, God-breathed, a single compound word found only here, teaching that the Bible originates in the breath of God and therefore carries His authority and truth. Because it is God-breathed it is ophelimos, profitable, useful for teaching, conviction, correction, and training in righteousness, doctrine and life together. The result is that the man of God is artios, complete and fitted, fully equipped for every good work, which is the Bible's own claim to sufficiency.

typology.type: The sacred writings known from infancy
typology.fulfillment: The Old Testament Scriptures that make one wise for salvation through faith in Christ, finding their goal in Him.

typology.type: The Lord's rescue of Paul from every persecution
typology.fulfillment: A foreshadowing of the final deliverance of all who suffer for godliness, secured in Christ.

study.christ: Christ is the saving goal of the Scriptures: they are able to make one wise for salvation through faith in Christ Jesus. All the sacred writings Timothy learned as a child were pointing to Him, and their wisdom is received only through faith in Him. The God-breathed word both testifies to Christ and equips His servants to do the good works He has prepared. To continue in the Scriptures, then, is to keep coming to Christ, in whom the whole Bible finds its meaning and its saving power.
study.god: God is the author of Scripture, which He breathes out as His own speech, and this is the ground of the Bible's authority, truthfulness, and profit. The same God who inspired the word governs the lives of His servants, rescuing Paul from every persecution and promising that godliness, though it invites suffering, is held in His hands. Through His breathed-out word God makes His people complete and furnishes them for every good work, so that His character and purposes are known and obeyed.

crossReferences.note: Scripture came not by human will but as men were carried along by the Holy Spirit, the companion text on inspiration.
The law of the Lord is perfect, reviving the soul, celebrating the profit of God's word.
Christ shows that all the Scriptures point to Him, grounding wisdom for salvation through faith in Him.
Timothy's sincere faith, first in Lois and Eunice, who taught him the sacred writings from infancy.

study.oneStory: God has spoken, breathing out Scripture so that His people might be made wise for salvation in Christ and equipped for His work. The same word Timothy learned at his grandmother's knee, the word Paul preached and suffered for, is the God-breathed word the church still holds. It runs through the whole story of redemption, leading generation after generation to Christ and fitting them for every good work until He comes.

sermon.title: Scripture: God-Breathed
sermon.why: Part of Begg's series A Study in 2 Timothy, this message expounds 2 Timothy 3:16, the claim that all Scripture is God-breathed, unfolding the Bible's divine origin, authority, and trustworthiness as the ground of the Christian's confidence in God's word.

quiz.q: What does Paul say Timothy should expect if he lives a godly life in Christ?

options.text: That he, like all who desire godliness in Christ, will be persecuted.
options.note: Verse 12 states plainly that all who desire to live godly will be persecuted.

options.text: That he will be spared all hardship and opposition.
options.note: The opposite: godliness in Christ invites persecution.

options.text: That only pastors and apostles face persecution.
options.note: Paul says all who desire godly lives, not only leaders.

options.text: That opposition is a normal part of following Christ faithfully.
options.note: True and close, generalizing the specific promise that the godly will be persecuted.

quiz.q: What does God-breathed mean in verse 16?

options.text: That Scripture originates from God, breathed out as His own word.
options.note: The compound word teaches the Bible's divine origin and authority.

options.text: That the Bible merely contains helpful human wisdom.
options.note: Paul roots Scripture in God's breath, not human insight.

options.text: That only some inspiring passages come from God.
options.note: Paul says all Scripture is God-breathed, not only select parts.

options.text: That the Bible carries God's own authority because He is its author.
options.note: True and close, drawing out the authority that follows from divine origin.

quiz.q: For what is Scripture profitable, according to verse 16?

options.text: Teaching, conviction, correction, and training in righteousness.
options.note: Verse 16 lists all four uses covering doctrine and life.

options.text: Only for private comfort, not for correcting error.
options.note: Paul explicitly includes conviction and correction.

options.text: Mainly for winning debates with opponents.
options.note: Its purpose is to equip for godliness and good works, not scoring points.

options.text: Shaping both what we believe and how we live.
options.note: True and close, summarizing the fourfold use of the word.

quiz.q: What is the goal of Scripture's work in a person, according to verse 17?

options.text: That the man of God be complete, fully equipped for every good work.
options.note: Verse 17 names completeness and equipping for good works as the aim.

options.text: That he simply accumulate more information.
options.note: The goal is not mere knowledge but a life equipped for good works.

options.text: That he feel proud of how much Bible he knows.
options.note: The aim is godly usefulness, not pride.

options.text: That he be made ready and able for the work God gives.
options.note: True and close, restating being complete and fully equipped.

quiz.q: Why does Paul point Timothy back to the Scriptures he knew from infancy?

options.text: Because they are able to make him wise for salvation through faith in Christ.
options.note: Verse 15 names their saving purpose, fulfilled in Christ.

options.text: Because childhood memories are pleasant to recall.
options.note: The point is the saving power of the Scriptures, not nostalgia.

options.text: Because old teaching is always better than new.
options.note: The issue is not age but that these are the God-breathed Scriptures leading to Christ.

options.text: Because they ground him in the truth that leads to salvation.
options.note: True and close, capturing their role in saving wisdom through faith in Christ.

questions: Timothy watched Paul's life up close, not just his teaching. Whose tested example of following Christ through hardship has shaped you, and whose example are you setting?
Paul says godliness invites persecution. Where has choosing to follow Christ faithfully cost you something, and how did the Lord meet you in it?
To continue in the Scriptures means abiding, not drifting toward novelty. What would it look like for you to settle down and stay rooted in God's word this season?
Scripture is God-breathed, His own word to us. How does remembering who the author is change the way you read and respond to the Bible?
The Bible aims to make you complete and equipped for every good work. Is there a good work God is preparing you for that His word is shaping you to do?

translationNotes.note: Antioch, Iconium, and Lystra recall Paul's sufferings on his first missionary journey, in the region Timothy came from, events Timothy would have known well.
Holy Scriptures renders sacred writings, a term for the Old Testament that Timothy was taught from infancy by his mother and grandmother.
God-breathed translates a single Greek compound found only here; some render the clause every God-breathed Scripture is also profitable, but both readings affirm divine inspiration and profit.
The four uses, rendered instruction, conviction, correction, and training in righteousness, together cover both sound doctrine and godly living.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 2 Timothy 3:10-17. No em dashes present in this movement's text.
